concept_name: Galvanometer → Ammeter & Voltmeter — shunt S = Ig·G/(I−Ig), series R = V/Ig − G
source_book: DC Pandey Class 12 Ch.4 Moving Charges and Magnetism §4.11 (Conversion of a galvanometer into an ammeter and a voltmeter) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. NCERT consulted for the Indian real-world anchor only. The atomic concept taught here is the CIRCUIT TOPOLOGY of converting a moving-coil galvanometer into a measuring instrument: a galvanometer carries only a tiny full-scale current Ig (microamp range) and has a coil resistance G, so it cannot directly read large currents or large voltages. To make an AMMETER, a SMALL shunt resistance S is wired in PARALLEL with the coil; the current splits so only Ig passes through the coil and the rest (I − Ig) bypasses through S. Because both branches join the same two nodes they share the same voltage, giving Ig·G = (I − Ig)·S, so S = Ig·G/(I − Ig) — a very small resistance; the combined G‖S is near zero, so an ammeter is connected in SERIES and ideally has ~0 Ω. To make a VOLTMETER, a LARGE resistance R is wired in SERIES with the coil; only Ig flows while the chain drops the full voltage V, giving R = V/Ig − G — a very large resistance; the combined G + R is near infinite, so a voltmeter is connected in PARALLEL across the component and ideally has ~∞ Ω. The deepest student confusions targeted: (M1) the galvanometer can directly read any current; (M2) the classic swap — ammeter in parallel / voltmeter in series; (M3) the shunt should be large; (M4) an ammeter should have high resistance; (M5) a voltmeter should have low resistance; (M6) adding a shunt changes what the coil reads (it always passes only Ig). CUT-LINE: this concept teaches the CONVERSION only — it does NOT re-derive how the galvanometer itself reads current (φ = (NAB/k)·I, current sensitivity NAB/k); that is the prerequisite concept moving_coil_galvanometer, which this re-uses, not re-derives. Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; the six misconceptions are confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch + predict→reveal).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the two meters on the bench in your school or college physics lab — the ammeter you put in SERIES to read the current through a resistor, and the voltmeter you clip ACROSS it to read the voltage. Both hide the very same moving coil you just studied; the only difference is a tiny resistor wired in. This concept is the wiring trick that turns one fragile galvanometer into either instrument.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the analog multimeter an electrician still carries, with a rotary dial that selects ranges — 250 mA, 10 A, 50 V, 250 V. Every click swaps in a different shunt or a different series resistance behind the same needle; the rotary switch is doing exactly the S = Ig·G/(I−Ig) and R = V/Ig − G arithmetic you will see here.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture the panel ammeter on an inverter or a battery charger and the voltmeter beside it. Same moving coil inside each, one wired with a small shunt to sit in the current line, the other wired with a large resistor to sit across the terminals.

formulas.current_split: Ig + I_shunt = I  — at the junction the current splits; the coil takes Ig, the shunt takes the rest.
formulas.equal_branch_voltage: Ig * G = (I - Ig) * S  — the parallel coil and shunt share the same voltage across the same two nodes.
formulas.shunt_resistance: S = Ig * G / (I - Ig)  — solve the equal-voltage condition; because I >> Ig, S is SMALL.
formulas.ammeter_resistance: G_A = G*S / (G + S) ~ 0  — coil in parallel with a small shunt: combined resistance near zero, so the ammeter goes in SERIES.
formulas.series_resistance: R = V / Ig - G  — only Ig flows while the chain drops the full V; because Ig is tiny, R is LARGE.
formulas.voltmeter_resistance: G_V = G + R ~ infinity  — coil in series with a large R: combined resistance near infinite, so the voltmeter goes in PARALLEL.

constraints: The galvanometer coil never carries more than its full-scale current Ig, no matter how large the measured current I is - the shunt carries the excess I - Ig.
Ammeter: the shunt S is wired in PARALLEL with the coil and is SMALL (S = Ig*G/(I-Ig)); the combined resistance G||S is near zero, so an ammeter is connected in SERIES.
Voltmeter: the resistance R is wired in SERIES with the coil and is LARGE (R = V/Ig - G); the combined resistance G + R is near infinite, so a voltmeter is connected in PARALLEL.
Swapping them breaks the circuit: a near-zero ammeter placed in parallel short-circuits the component; a near-infinite voltmeter placed in series blocks the current.
An ideal ammeter has zero resistance (no voltage drop across it); an ideal voltmeter has infinite resistance (draws no current).
This concept re-uses, not re-derives, that the galvanometer reads current via phi = (NAB/k)*I (prerequisite moving_coil_galvanometer); here Ig and G are taken as given device constants.

aha_moment.statement: A galvanometer plus a small parallel shunt is a near-zero-ohm ammeter, wired in series.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: STATE_5 cross-fades the coil and its small shunt into a single 'A' meter box that drops into the main series line, with a '≈ 0 Ω' badge, while the full current sails through undisturbed.

STATE_1.title: The delicate galvanometer: tiny Ig, coil resistance G

scene_composition.text: A galvanometer — symbol G — the moving coil you already studied

Amber dots = current creeping
through the delicate coil.
It reads only a tiny full-scale
current Ig, and has resistance G.

Like the bare meter movement
inside the ammeter and the
voltmeter on your lab bench —
before any resistor is wired in.
Question: how do we turn this fragile detector into an ammeter and a voltmeter?

tts_sentences.text_en: This is a galvanometer, the symbol G. Watch the current creep through its delicate coil.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह एक galvanometer है, symbol G। देखिए current इसकी delicate coil में धीरे-धीरे बहता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది ఒక galvanometer, symbol G. దాని delicate coil గుండా current నెమ్మదిగా ప్రవహించడాన్ని గమనించండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: It reads only a tiny full-scale current, the microamp-level current I g, and its coil has resistance G.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह केवल एक tiny full-scale current पढ़ता है, microamp-level current I g, और इसकी coil की resistance G है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది కేవలం ఒక tiny full-scale current ను చదువుతుంది, microamp-level current I g, మరియు దాని coil కి resistance G ఉంటుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: So how do we turn this fragile detector into an ammeter and a voltmeter?
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो हम इस fragile detector को ammeter और voltmeter में कैसे बदलें?
tts_sentences.text_te: మరి ఈ fragile detector ను ammeter మరియు voltmeter గా ఎలా మారుస్తాం?

misconception_watch.belief: A galvanometer can directly measure any current or voltage you connect it to.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The current is shown creeping through the coil, with labels that it survives only the tiny full-scale current Ig and carries a coil resistance G.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A galvanometer only handles a tiny full-scale current Ig; reading large currents or voltages needs an added resistor.

STATE_2.title: The problem: a real circuit would burn the bare coil

scene_composition.text: Drop the bare galvanometer into a real circuit: battery + load

A real circuit can push
whole amperes. The coil only
survives the tiny Ig.
What happens to the needle?

The current floods through —
the needle slams off-scale and
the coil would burn out.
Across a big voltage V: same.
The bare galvanometer cannot read large I or large V — we must protect and rescale it

tts_sentences.text_en: Drop the bare galvanometer into a real circuit with a battery and a load.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Bare galvanometer को एक real circuit में डालिए, battery और load के साथ।
tts_sentences.text_te: Bare galvanometer ను ఒక real circuit లో ఉంచండి, battery మరియు load తో.

tts_sentences.text_en: A real circuit can push whole amperes, but the coil only survives the tiny current I g.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक real circuit पूरे amperes push कर सकता है, लेकिन coil केवल tiny current I g सहती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక real circuit మొత్తం amperes ను push చేయగలదు, కానీ coil కేవలం tiny current I g ను మాత్రమే తట్టుకుంటుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Send the full current through it and the needle slams off-scale; the coil would burn out.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसमें full current भेजिए और needle off-scale slam हो जाती है; coil burn out हो जाती।
tts_sentences.text_te: దానిలో full current పంపండి, needle off-scale కి కొట్టుకుంటుంది; coil burn out అయిపోతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: And across a large voltage it draws too much current to read. We must protect and rescale it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: और एक large voltage के across यह पढ़ने के लिए बहुत ज़्यादा current खींचती है। हमें इसे protect और rescale करना होगा।
tts_sentences.text_te: మరియు ఒక large voltage అంతటా ఇది చదవడానికి చాలా ఎక్కువ current ను లాగుతుంది. మనం దీన్ని protect మరియు rescale చేయాలి.

misconception_watch.belief: You can just connect the galvanometer in any circuit and read whatever current or voltage is there.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: In a battery-and-load circuit the current floods fast through the coil and the off-scale warning fires — the bare coil cannot take it.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A real circuit pushes far more than Ig, so the bare coil would burn out — it must be protected and rescaled with an added resistor.

STATE_3.title: Ammeter idea: a small shunt in parallel splits the current

scene_composition.text: To read a large current I, add a small shunt S in parallel with G

Predict before the reveal:
when the current reaches the
junction, how does it split
between the coil and the shunt?

Most of it races through the
low-resistance shunt; only the
tiny Ig still passes through
the coil — the rest is I − Ig.
The coil never carries more than Ig — the shunt safely carries the excess I − Ig

tts_sentences.text_en: To measure a large current, we add a small shunt resistance S in parallel with the galvanometer.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक large current measure करने के लिए, हम galvanometer के parallel एक small shunt resistance S जोड़ते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక large current ను measure చేయడానికి, galvanometer కి parallel గా ఒక small shunt resistance S జోడిస్తాం.

tts_sentences.text_en: Predict: when the current reaches the junction, how does it split between the coil and the shunt?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Predict कीजिए: जब current junction पर पहुँचता है, यह coil और shunt के बीच कैसे split होता है?
tts_sentences.text_te: Predict చేయండి: current junction కి చేరినప్పుడు, అది coil మరియు shunt మధ్య ఎలా split అవుతుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: Most of it races through the low-resistance shunt; only the tiny current I g still passes through the coil.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसका अधिकांश low-resistance shunt से race करता है; केवल tiny current I g अभी भी coil से गुज़रती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: దానిలో ఎక్కువ భాగం low-resistance shunt గుండా race చేస్తుంది; కేవలం tiny current I g మాత్రమే coil గుండా వెళుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: The galvanometer never carries more than I g; the shunt safely carries the rest, the current I minus I g.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Galvanometer कभी भी I g से ज़्यादा नहीं ले जाती; shunt बाकी को safely ले जाती है, current I minus I g।
tts_sentences.text_te: Galvanometer ఎప్పుడూ I g కంటే ఎక్కువ తీసుకువెళ్ళదు; shunt మిగిలినదాన్ని safely తీసుకువెళుతుంది, current I minus I g.

misconception_watch.belief: Adding a shunt changes how much current the galvanometer coil reads.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: At the junction the dots split: a thick fast stream through the shunt and only a tiny trickle through the coil, labelled Ig vs I − Ig.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The coil always carries only Ig; the shunt simply bypasses the excess current I − Ig, so the coil is never overloaded.

STATE_4.title: The shunt formula: S = Ig·G/(I−Ig) is small

scene_composition.text: Both branches join the same two nodes — so they share the same voltage

Equal voltage across the two
parallel branches:
Ig · G  =  (I − Ig) · S.
Solve for the shunt S.

S = Ig · G / (I − Ig).
Because I is much larger
than Ig, the shunt S comes
out VERY small (≈ 0.1 Ω).
Ig·G = (I − Ig)·S   →   S = Ig·G/(I − Ig)   (small)

tts_sentences.text_en: Both branches connect the same two junctions, so they share the same voltage across them.
tts_sentences.text_hi: दोनों branches एक ही दो junctions को connect करती हैं, इसलिए वे same voltage share करती हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండు branches ఒకే రెండు junctions ను connect చేస్తాయి, కాబట్టి అవి same voltage ను share చేస్తాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: That gives current I g times G equals the shunt current times S — equal voltage on the two parallel branches.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इससे मिलता है current I g times G equals shunt current times S — दोनों parallel branches पर equal voltage।
tts_sentences.text_te: దీని నుండి current I g times G equals shunt current times S వస్తుంది — రెండు parallel branches పై equal voltage.

tts_sentences.text_en: Solve it and the shunt resistance S equals I g times G, divided by I minus I g.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसे solve कीजिए और shunt resistance S equals I g times G, divided by I minus I g।
tts_sentences.text_te: దాన్ని solve చేయండి, shunt resistance S equals I g times G, divided by I minus I g.

tts_sentences.text_en: Because the current I is much larger than I g, the shunt resistance S comes out very small.
tts_sentences.text_hi: क्योंकि current I, I g से बहुत बड़ा है, shunt resistance S बहुत small आती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Current I, I g కంటే చాలా పెద్దది కాబట్టి, shunt resistance S చాలా small గా వస్తుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: The shunt resistance should be large, like an ordinary resistor.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The equal-voltage condition resolves to S = Ig·G/(I−Ig), and the shunt is labelled with a small value (≈ 0.1 Ω) beside the large current it diverts.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Because I is much larger than Ig, S = Ig·G/(I−Ig) is very small — a shunt is a near-zero resistance, not a large one.

STATE_5.title: Ideal ammeter ~0 Ω, connected in series

scene_composition.text: Seal the coil and its shunt into one unit — the ammeter, marked A

The galvanometer and a tiny
shunt sit in parallel.
Is their combined resistance
large, or small?

Tiny — close to zero ohms.
So the ammeter barely disturbs
the circuit, and is wired in
SERIES, in the current's path.
Ammeter = G ‖ small S ≈ 0 Ω → connect in SERIES (ideal ammeter has zero resistance)

tts_sentences.text_en: Seal the coil and its shunt into one unit; that is your ammeter, marked A.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Coil और इसके shunt को एक unit में seal कीजिए; वह है आपका ammeter, marked A।
tts_sentences.text_te: Coil మరియు దాని shunt ను ఒక unit గా seal చేయండి; అదే మీ ammeter, marked A.

tts_sentences.text_en: Predict: the galvanometer and a tiny shunt sit in parallel. Is their combined resistance large or small?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Predict कीजिए: galvanometer और एक tiny shunt parallel में हैं। उनकी combined resistance large है या small?
tts_sentences.text_te: Predict చేయండి: galvanometer మరియు ఒక tiny shunt parallel లో ఉన్నాయి. వాటి combined resistance large గా ఉందా, small గా ఉందా?

tts_sentences.text_en: It is tiny, close to zero ohms, so the ammeter barely disturbs the circuit it measures.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह tiny है, zero ohms के करीब, इसलिए ammeter जिस circuit को measure करता है उसे barely disturb करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది tiny, zero ohms కి దగ్గరగా, కాబట్టి ammeter తాను measure చేసే circuit ను barely disturb చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: That is why an ammeter is connected in series, and an ideal ammeter has almost zero resistance.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसीलिए ammeter series में connect होता है, और ideal ammeter की resistance लगभग zero होती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: అందుకే ammeter series లో connect అవుతుంది, మరియు ideal ammeter resistance దాదాపు zero గా ఉంటుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: An ammeter should have a high resistance, and it can go anywhere in the circuit.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The coil and small shunt cross-fade into one 'A' box with a '≈ 0 Ω' badge that drops into the series line, the full current passing straight through.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A galvanometer in parallel with a small shunt is near zero ohms, so an ammeter is wired in series and ideally has zero resistance.

STATE_6.title: Voltmeter idea: a large R in series; R = V/Ig − G

scene_composition.text: To read a voltage V, add a large resistance R in series with G

With this big resistance in
the line, how much current
flows through the coil —
a lot, or just Ig?

Only the tiny Ig flows: the
large R chokes the current to
the coil's full-scale value.
The chain drops the whole V.
V = Ig·(G + R)   →   R = V/Ig − G   (large)

tts_sentences.text_en: Now to read a voltage, we add a large resistance R in series with the galvanometer.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब एक voltage पढ़ने के लिए, हम galvanometer के series में एक large resistance R जोड़ते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు ఒక voltage చదవడానికి, galvanometer కి series లో ఒక large resistance R జోడిస్తాం.

tts_sentences.text_en: Predict: with this big resistance in the line, how much current flows through the coil?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Predict कीजिए: line में इस big resistance के साथ, coil से कितना current बहता है?
tts_sentences.text_te: Predict చేయండి: line లో ఈ big resistance తో, coil గుండా ఎంత current ప్రవహిస్తుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: Only the tiny current I g flows, because the large R chokes the current to the galvanometer's full-scale value.
tts_sentences.text_hi: केवल tiny current I g बहता है, क्योंकि large R current को galvanometer के full-scale value तक choke कर देती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కేవలం tiny current I g మాత్రమే ప్రవహిస్తుంది, ఎందుకంటే large R current ను galvanometer full-scale value కి choke చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: The chain drops the whole voltage at that current, so the series resistance R equals V over I g, minus G.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Chain उस current पर पूरा voltage drop करती है, इसलिए series resistance R equals V over I g, minus G।
tts_sentences.text_te: Chain ఆ current వద్ద మొత్తం voltage ను drop చేస్తుంది, కాబట్టి series resistance R equals V over I g, minus G.

misconception_watch.belief: To make a voltmeter you add a small resistance, or the series resistor should be small.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A large zigzag R appears in series with G, only a thin Ig trickle creeps through, and R is resolved as R = V/Ig − G with a large value.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A voltmeter adds a LARGE series resistance R = V/Ig − G, so only the tiny Ig flows while the chain drops the full voltage V.

STATE_7.title: Ideal voltmeter ~∞ Ω, connected in parallel

scene_composition.text: Seal the coil and its large R into one unit — the voltmeter, marked V

The coil plus a big resistance
R add up to a very large
combined resistance.
How much current can it draw?

Almost none — ideally infinite
resistance. So it barely
disturbs the circuit, and sits
in PARALLEL, across the part.
Voltmeter = G + large R ≈ ∞ Ω → connect in PARALLEL (ideal voltmeter draws no current)

tts_sentences.text_en: Seal the coil and its large series resistance into one unit; that is your voltmeter, marked V.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Coil और इसकी large series resistance को एक unit में seal कीजिए; वह है आपका voltmeter, marked V।
tts_sentences.text_te: Coil మరియు దాని large series resistance ను ఒక unit గా seal చేయండి; అదే మీ voltmeter, marked V.

tts_sentences.text_en: The galvanometer plus a big resistance R add up to a very large combined resistance.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Galvanometer plus एक big resistance R मिलकर एक बहुत large combined resistance बनाते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Galvanometer plus ఒక big resistance R కలిసి చాలా large combined resistance గా అవుతాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: So the voltmeter draws almost no current, ideally infinite resistance, and barely disturbs the circuit.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसलिए voltmeter लगभग कोई current नहीं खींचता, ideally infinite resistance, और circuit को barely disturb करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి voltmeter దాదాపు ఏ current ను లాగదు, ideally infinite resistance, మరియు circuit ను barely disturb చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: That is why a voltmeter is connected in parallel, straight across the part whose voltage you want.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसीलिए voltmeter parallel में connect होता है, उस part के across जिसका voltage आप चाहते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: అందుకే voltmeter parallel లో connect అవుతుంది, మీకు కావలసిన voltage ఉన్న part అంతటా.

misconception_watch.belief: A voltmeter should have a low resistance, and it can be put in series like an ammeter.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The coil and large R cross-fade into one 'V' box with a '≈ ∞ Ω' badge that hops across the load, drawing only a near-zero trickle.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A galvanometer in series with a large R is near infinite ohms, so a voltmeter is wired in parallel and ideally draws no current.

STATE_8.title: Ammeter vs voltmeter — the swap that fails

scene_composition.text: Ammeter in series (≈ 0 Ω); voltmeter in parallel (≈ ∞ Ω)

What if you swapped them —
an ammeter across a part,
a voltmeter in the line?
Predict what breaks.

A near-zero ammeter across a
part short-circuits it; a near-
infinite voltmeter in series
blocks the current. Both fail.
Ammeter: series + small shunt.   Voltmeter: parallel + large series R.   Never swap them.

tts_sentences.text_en: Side by side: the ammeter sits in series with almost zero resistance; the voltmeter sits across, with almost infinite resistance.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Side by side: ammeter series में almost zero resistance के साथ है; voltmeter across में almost infinite resistance के साथ है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Side by side: ammeter series లో almost zero resistance తో ఉంటుంది; voltmeter across లో almost infinite resistance తో ఉంటుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Swap them and the circuit breaks: a near-zero ammeter across a component short-circuits it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इन्हें swap कीजिए और circuit टूट जाता है: एक component के across near-zero ammeter उसे short-circuit कर देता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: వీటిని swap చేస్తే circuit విరిగిపోతుంది: ఒక component అంతటా near-zero ammeter దాన్ని short-circuit చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: And a near-infinite voltmeter placed in series blocks the current almost completely.
tts_sentences.text_hi: और series में रखा एक near-infinite voltmeter current को लगभग पूरी तरह block कर देता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మరియు series లో ఉంచిన ఒక near-infinite voltmeter current ను దాదాపు పూర్తిగా block చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Ammeter in series with a small shunt; voltmeter in parallel with a large series resistance — never the other way round.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Ammeter series में small shunt के साथ; voltmeter parallel में large series resistance के साथ — कभी उल्टा नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Ammeter series లో small shunt తో; voltmeter parallel లో large series resistance తో — ఎప్పుడూ తలకిందులుగా కాదు.

misconception_watch.belief: An ammeter and a voltmeter are interchangeable — it does not matter how you connect them.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Both correct meters run side by side with the swap warning pulsing; the swap shorts or blocks the circuit, so the dots stall.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: An ammeter must be in series (≈ 0 Ω) and a voltmeter in parallel (≈ ∞ Ω); swapping them short-circuits or blocks the circuit.

STATE_9.title: Your turn — Ig, G, I, V → S, R

scene_composition.text: Explore: drag Ig, coil resistance G, current range I, voltage range V

Watch the shunt S and the
series R update live as you
change the ranges. S stays
small; R stays large.

Challenge:
Raise the current range I —
does the shunt S shrink?
Raise V — does R grow?
S = Ig·G/(I − Ig) (small, parallel) · R = V/Ig − G (large, series)

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Drag the full-scale current I g, the coil resistance G, the current range I, and the voltage range V.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आपकी बारी। Drag कीजिए full-scale current I g, coil resistance G, current range I, और voltage range V।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీ వంతు. Drag చేయండి full-scale current I g, coil resistance G, current range I, మరియు voltage range V.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the shunt resistance S and the series resistance R update live as you change the ranges.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Shunt resistance S और series resistance R को live update होते देखिए जैसे आप ranges बदलते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Shunt resistance S మరియు series resistance R ను live update అవుతూ చూడండి, మీరు ranges మార్చుతున్నప్పుడు.

tts_sentences.text_en: Notice the shunt S stays small for an ammeter, while the series R stays large for a voltmeter.
tts_sentences.text_hi: देखिए shunt S एक ammeter के लिए small रहती है, जबकि series R एक voltmeter के लिए large रहती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: గమనించండి shunt S ఒక ammeter కోసం small గా ఉంటుంది, అయితే series R ఒక voltmeter కోసం large గా ఉంటుంది.

STATE_1.label: A galvanometer alone — tiny Ig creeping through the coil
STATE_1.caption: A galvanometer (symbol G): a delicate coil that reads only a tiny full-scale current Ig and has resistance G.

STATE_2.label: Bare galvanometer in a real circuit — the current floods
STATE_2.caption: Put the bare galvanometer in a real battery-and-load circuit: the full current floods through and would burn the coil.

STATE_3.label: Ammeter idea: small shunt S in parallel — the current splits
STATE_3.caption: Wire a small shunt S in parallel with G: at the junction the current splits — only Ig through the coil, I − Ig through the shunt.

STATE_4.label: Shunt formula: equal branch voltage → S = Ig·G/(I−Ig), small
STATE_4.caption: Both branches share the same voltage: Ig·G = (I − Ig)·S, so S = Ig·G/(I − Ig) — a very small resistance (≈ 0.1 Ω here).

STATE_5.label: Assemble the ammeter: G ‖ small S ≈ 0 Ω, dropped into the series line
STATE_5.caption: Coil + small shunt seal into one 'A' meter (≈ 0 Ω) dropped into the series line; the full current sails through undisturbed.

STATE_6.label: Voltmeter idea: large R in series — only Ig flows; R = V/Ig − G
STATE_6.caption: Wire a large R in series with G across the load: only the tiny Ig creeps through, and the chain drops the full V — so R = V/Ig − G.

STATE_7.label: Assemble the voltmeter: G + large R ≈ ∞ Ω, hopped across the load
STATE_7.caption: Coil + large R seal into one 'V' meter (≈ ∞ Ω) sitting across the load; it draws only a near-zero trickle, in parallel.

STATE_8.label: Compare: ammeter in series (≈ 0 Ω) vs voltmeter in parallel (≈ ∞ Ω) — swap breaks it
STATE_8.caption: The correct picture: 'A' in series (≈ 0 Ω), 'V' in parallel (≈ ∞ Ω). Swap them and the circuit short-circuits or blocks.

STATE_9.label: Sandbox — Ig, G, I, V sliders with a live S + R readout
STATE_9.caption: Drag Ig, G, I, V. Shunt S = Ig·G/(I − Ig) and series R = V/Ig − G update live: S stays small, R stays large.

regeneration_variants.label: Analog multimeter range switch
regeneration_variants.physical_world: The rotary range switch on an electrician's analog multimeter (250 mA / 10 A / 50 V / 250 V)
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Each click wires a different shunt S = Ig·G/(I−Ig) or series R = V/Ig − G behind the same moving coil, rescaling it to a new current or voltage range
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A dial selecting ranges, the same coil behind it, a small shunt swapping in for current ranges and a large series resistor for voltage ranges.

regeneration_variants.label: Panel ammeter and voltmeter on an inverter
regeneration_variants.physical_world: The panel ammeter and voltmeter side by side on a home inverter or battery charger
regeneration_variants.same_physics: The ammeter hides a small shunt in parallel and sits in the current line (≈ 0 Ω); the voltmeter hides a large series resistor and sits across the terminals (≈ ∞ Ω)
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Two panel meters, one wired into the current line with a small shunt, the other clipped across the terminals with a large series resistor.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered this concept when they can explain that a moving-coil galvanometer carries only a tiny full-scale current Ig and has a coil resistance G, so it cannot directly read large currents or voltages; that to build an AMMETER a SMALL shunt resistance S is wired in PARALLEL with the coil so the current splits, only Ig passing through the coil and I − Ig bypassing through S, and that the equal voltage across the two branches gives Ig·G = (I − Ig)·S, so S = Ig·G/(I − Ig) — a small resistance; that the combined resistance G‖S is near zero, which is why an ammeter is connected in SERIES and an ideal ammeter has zero resistance; that to build a VOLTMETER a LARGE resistance R is wired in SERIES with the coil so only Ig flows while the chain drops the full voltage V, giving R = V/Ig − G — a large resistance; that the combined resistance G + R is near infinite, which is why a voltmeter is connected in PARALLEL across the component and an ideal voltmeter draws no current; and that swapping them breaks the circuit, because a near-zero ammeter in parallel short-circuits the component and a near-infinite voltmeter in series blocks the current.

questions.stem: To measure a large current with a galvanometer, what do you connect, and where?

options.A: A small shunt resistance in parallel, so most of the current bypasses the coil and only Ig flows through it
options.B: A large resistance in series, so the coil shares the current with it
options.C: A small resistance in series, to drop the extra current before it reaches the coil
options.D: A large resistance in parallel, so the coil takes most of the current

distractor_misconceptions.B: Confuses the ammeter conversion with the voltmeter conversion (large series R)
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the protecting resistor goes in series, when an ammeter needs a parallel shunt
distractor_misconceptions.D: Gets parallel right but thinks the shunt is large, sending most current through the coil

questions.tested_idea: An ammeter is a galvanometer with a small shunt in parallel; the coil still carries only Ig.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A galvanometer must read up to 5 A but its coil takes only 1 mA. What kind of resistor do you add, and is it in series or parallel?

questions.stem: In the ammeter, why are the shunt and the galvanometer said to have the same voltage, and what does that give for the shunt S?

options.A: They join the same two nodes, so Ig·G = (I − Ig)·S, giving S = Ig·G/(I − Ig), a small resistance
options.B: Because they carry the same current, so S must equal G
options.C: Because the shunt is large, the voltage across it dominates the coil's
options.D: S = (I − Ig)·G/Ig, which works out to be large

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks parallel branches carry equal current rather than share equal voltage
distractor_misconceptions.C: Assumes the shunt is large, the opposite of the small S that the equal-voltage condition gives
distractor_misconceptions.D: Inverts the formula, producing a large shunt instead of the correct small one

questions.tested_idea: Equal voltage on the parallel branches gives S = Ig·G/(I − Ig), which is small.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A coil (Ig, G) and a shunt S are wired between the same two points. Write the condition that fixes S and say whether S is large or small.

questions.stem: An ammeter is a galvanometer with a small shunt in parallel. What is its overall resistance, and how is it connected in a circuit?

options.A: Very small, near zero, so it is connected in series and barely disturbs the circuit
options.B: Very large, so it is connected in parallel across the component
options.C: Equal to the coil resistance G, and connected in series
options.D: Near zero, so it is connected in parallel across the component

distractor_misconceptions.B: Describes a voltmeter (large resistance, parallel), not an ammeter
distractor_misconceptions.C: Ignores the shunt, which pulls the combined resistance far below G toward zero
distractor_misconceptions.D: Gets the near-zero resistance right but places it in parallel, which would short-circuit the component

questions.tested_idea: A galvanometer in parallel with a small shunt gives ~0 Ω, so an ammeter goes in series.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A coil in parallel with a tiny shunt forms an ammeter. State its approximate resistance and whether it goes in series or parallel, with the reason.

questions.stem: To convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter that reads up to a voltage V, what do you add, and what is its value?

options.A: A large resistance R in series with the coil, with R = V/Ig − G
options.B: A small shunt in parallel with the coil, with S = Ig·G/(I − Ig)
options.C: A large resistance in parallel with the coil, with R = V/Ig
options.D: A small resistance in series with the coil, with R = V·Ig

distractor_misconceptions.B: Confuses the voltmeter conversion with the ammeter shunt
distractor_misconceptions.C: Gets large-R right but places it in parallel and drops the −G correction
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks the series resistor is small and uses a wrong formula

questions.tested_idea: A voltmeter is a galvanometer with a large series resistance R = V/Ig − G.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A galvanometer (Ig, G) must read up to 100 V. What resistor do you add, in series or parallel, and what value?

questions.stem: What is the overall resistance of a good voltmeter, and how is it connected in a circuit?

options.A: Very large, near infinite, so it is connected in parallel and draws almost no current
options.B: Very small, so it is connected in series in the current's path
options.C: Equal to the coil resistance G, connected in parallel
options.D: Near infinite, so it is connected in series so that no current flows

distractor_misconceptions.B: Describes an ammeter (small resistance, series), not a voltmeter
distractor_misconceptions.C: Ignores the large series R, which raises the combined resistance far above G
distractor_misconceptions.D: Gets the near-infinite resistance right but places it in series, which would block the circuit

questions.tested_idea: A galvanometer plus a large series R is ~∞ Ω, so a voltmeter goes in parallel.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A coil in series with a large resistor forms a voltmeter. State its approximate resistance and whether it goes in series or parallel, with the reason.

questions.stem: A student connects the ammeter in parallel across a resistor and the voltmeter in series in the line. What happens?

options.A: Both fail: the near-zero ammeter short-circuits the resistor, and the near-infinite voltmeter blocks the current
options.B: Nothing changes — meters work correctly in any position
options.C: Only the ammeter fails; the voltmeter still reads correctly in series
options.D: Both still read, but each shows exactly double its true value

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks meter placement is arbitrary, ignoring that ammeters are low-resistance and voltmeters high-resistance
distractor_misconceptions.C: Accepts a voltmeter in series, missing that its near-infinite resistance blocks the current
distractor_misconceptions.D: Invents a factor-of-two error instead of recognising a broken circuit

questions.tested_idea: An ammeter must be in series (low R) and a voltmeter in parallel (high R); swapping them breaks the circuit.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Explain, in terms of each meter's resistance, why putting an ammeter in parallel and a voltmeter in series damages or disables the measurement.
